edges.target: Defective Fas-mediated apoptosis of activated lymphocytes

nodes.description: Elevated circulating alpha-beta double-negative T cells are a characteristic immunophenotypic marker of FAS-related ALPS.

evidence.reference_title: Investigation of common variable immunodeficiency patients and healthy individuals using autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: Biomarkers including elevated CD3+TCRαβ+CD4-CD8- double negative T cells (TCRαβ+ DNT), IL-10, sCD95L and vitamin B12 can be used to differentiate between ALPS and common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) patients with an overlapping clinical phenotype.

nodes.description: Immune-mediated red-cell destruction is one of the most common autoimmune cytopenias in ALPS.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Cytopenias in these patients can be the result of splenic sequestration as well as autoimmune complications manifesting as autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, and autoimmune neutropenia.

nodes.description: Autoimmune neutropenia occurs as part of the multilineage cytopenia spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Cytopenias in these patients can be the result of splenic sequestration as well as autoimmune complications manifesting as autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, and autoimmune neutropenia.

nodes.description: Immune-mediated platelet destruction is a recurrent hematologic complication of FAS-related ALPS.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Cytopenias in these patients can be the result of splenic sequestration as well as autoimmune complications manifesting as autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, and autoimmune neutropenia.

nodes.description: Because activated lymphocytes are not efficiently deleted, lymphoid mass accumulates and the characteristic circulating alpha-beta double-negative T cell population expands. Clinically this drives chronic, non-infectious, non-malignant lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly.
nodes.id: Chronic lymphocyte accumulation and double-negative T-cell expansion

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) represents a failure of apoptotic mechanisms to maintain lymphocyte homeostasis, permitting accumulation of lymphoid mass and persistence of autoreactive cells that often manifest in childhood with chronic nonmalignant lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and recurring multilineage cytopenias.

evidence.reference_title: Sequential decisions on FAS sequencing guided by biomarkers in patients with lymphoproliferation and autoimmune cytopenia.
evidence.snippet: Among 98 patients sequenced for FAS mutations in CD3(+)TCRα/β(+)CD4(-)CD8(-) "double negative" T cells, 32 had germline and six had somatic FAS mutations.

nodes.description: Short corticosteroid pulses are used for acute or severe autoimmune cytopenia flares and selected severe lymphoproliferative complications, but chronic corticosteroid exposure should be minimized by steroid-sparing therapy when prolonged treatment is required.
nodes.id: Corticosteroid pulses for acute autoimmune cytopenias

evidence.reference_title: New advances in the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Most patients respond to short corticosteroid pulses.

nodes.description: Hypogammaglobulinemia is not the defining untreated immune phenotype, but it can occur in selected ALPS patients, particularly after B-cell depletion, and can drive immunoglobulin replacement needs.

evidence.reference_title: Use of rituximab for refractory cytopenias associated with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS).
evidence.snippet: Noted toxicities included profound and prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia in three patients requiring replacement IVIG

nodes.description: Pathogenic FAS variants impair signaling through the Fas death receptor and blunt activation-induced cell death of mature lymphocytes. This failure of peripheral deletion is the core molecular lesion underlying FAS-related ALPS.
nodes.id: Defective Fas-mediated apoptosis of activated lymphocytes

biological_processes: activation-induced cell death of T cells
extrinsic apoptotic signaling pathway via death domain receptors

evidence.reference_title: [Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome: molecular diagnosis in two families].
evidence.snippet: In family A, in vitro Fas-mediated apoptosis was absent in the patient and markedly reduced in his father.

evidence.reference_title: New advances in the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) is a disorder of disrupted lymphocyte homeostasis, resulting from mutations in the Fas apoptotic pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Sequential decisions on FAS sequencing guided by biomarkers in patients with lymphoproliferation and autoimmune cytopenia.
evidence.snippet: Clinical and genetic heterogeneity renders confirmation or exclusion of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome difficult. To re-evaluate and improve the currently suggested diagnostic approach to patients with suspected FAS mutation, the most frequent cause of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome, we prospectively determined 11 biomarkers in 163 patients with splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy and presumed or proven autoimmune cytopenia(s).

evidence.reference_title: [Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome: molecular diagnosis in two families].
evidence.snippet: Both mutations are located in exon 9 of TNFRSF6 gene, affecting the death domain of the Fas protein.

evidence.reference_title: New advances in the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: A number of key observations have been made recently that better define the pathophysiology of ALPS, including the characterization of somatic FAS variant ALPS, the identification of haploinsufficiency as a mechanism of decreased Fas expression, and the description of multiple genetic hits in FAS in some families that may explain the variable penetrance of the disease.

nodes.description: Persistent enlargement of the liver and spleen reflects chronic benign lymphoid accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) represents a failure of apoptotic mechanisms to maintain lymphocyte homeostasis, permitting accumulation of lymphoid mass and persistence of autoreactive cells that often manifest in childhood with chronic nonmalignant lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and recurring multilineage cytopenias.

nodes.description: Immunoglobulin infusion is considered for clinically significant hypogammaglobulinemia or as adjunctive therapy in severe autoimmune cytopenia contexts, particularly when B-cell depletion has caused prolonged antibody deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Use of rituximab for refractory cytopenias associated with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS).
evidence.snippet: profound and prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia in three patients requiring replacement IVIG

nodes.description: Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia is a supportive immune-laboratory feature in ALPS, especially when combined with autoimmune cytopenias, lymphoproliferation, and elevated double-negative T cells.

evidence.reference_title: New advances in the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our group established that a combination of autoimmune cytopenias and hypergammaglobulinemia are very predictive of ALPS in patients with lymphoproliferation and elevated DNTs.

nodes.description: Chronic, non-malignant lymph node enlargement is a defining clinical manifestation of FAS-related ALPS.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) represents a failure of apoptotic mechanisms to maintain lymphocyte homeostasis, permitting accumulation of lymphoid mass and persistence of autoreactive cells that often manifest in childhood with chronic nonmalignant lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and recurring multilineage cytopenias.

nodes.description: FAS-related ALPS confers increased lifetime risk of both Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

evidence.reference_title: Development of lymphoma in Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (ALPS) and its relationship to Fas gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with germline mutations in the Fas gene have a high risk to develop non Hodgkin lymphomas (x 14) as well as Hodgkin lymphomas (x 51), in particular NLP Hodgkin lymphoma.

nodes.description: Defective Fas-mediated apoptosis also weakens deletion of transformed or transformation-prone lymphocytes, creating a lifelong predisposition to lymphoma, especially B-cell and Hodgkin-type malignancies.

biological_processes: extrinsic apoptotic signaling pathway via death domain receptors

evidence.reference_title: Development of lymphoma in Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (ALPS) and its relationship to Fas gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with germline mutations in the Fas gene have a high risk to develop non Hodgkin lymphomas (x 14) as well as Hodgkin lymphomas (x 51), in particular NLP Hodgkin lymphoma.

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Some of these patients with FAS mutations affecting the intracellular portion of the FAS protein also have an increased risk of B-cell lymphoma.

nodes.description: Lifelong clinical surveillance for lymphoma should focus on changes in disease pattern, constitutional symptoms, suspicious nodes or masses, CBC trends, LDH, and targeted imaging or biopsy when clinically indicated rather than routine serial PET/CT for stable benign lymphoproliferation.

evidence.reference_title: New advances in the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Rather, we investigate for malignancy if patients develop constitutional symptoms or have a significant change in disease pattern.

nodes.description: Failed deletion of activated lymphocytes allows autoreactive clones to persist, breaking peripheral tolerance and producing immune-mediated destruction of multiple hematopoietic lineages.
nodes.id: Persistence of autoreactive lymphocytes and autoimmune cytopenias

biological_processes: tolerance induction dependent upon immune response

evidence.reference_title: How I treat autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Cytopenias in these patients can be the result of splenic sequestration as well as autoimmune complications manifesting as autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, and autoimmune neutropenia.

evidence.reference_title: The autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome: A rare disorder providing clues about normal tolerance.
evidence.snippet: While ALPS is one of the few autoimmune diseases with a known genetic defect, there remain unanswered questions regarding how a defect in apoptosis results in the observed phenotype.

nodes.description: If splenectomy has already occurred or is unavoidable, infection-prevention planning should include penicillin prophylaxis, pneumococcal and other asplenia-appropriate vaccination, and urgent evaluation of febrile illness.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome - GeneReviews® - NCBI Bookshelf
evidence.snippet: Vaccinations pre-splenectomy (with consideration of post-splenectomy boost vaccinations) and penicillin prophylaxis are strongly recommended for individuals who undergo splenectomy.

nodes.description: Severe infection risk is especially important after splenectomy or treatment-related hypogammaglobulinemia; this context drives explicit splenectomy avoidance and infection-prevention planning.

evidence.reference_title: Natural history of autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome associated with FAS gene mutations.
evidence.snippet: the major causes of morbidity and mortality in these patients are the overwhelming postsplenectomy sepsis and development of lymphoma.

nodes.description: Rituximab can be considered only after other immunosuppressive strategies fail or are not tolerated, because ALPS-specific toxicity includes prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia, impaired polysaccharide vaccine responses, and added infection risk, especially in asplenic patients.

evidence.reference_title: Use of rituximab for refractory cytopenias associated with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS).
evidence.snippet: Seven out of 12 patients responded with stabilization of cytopenia for greater than 6 months.

evidence.reference_title: Use of rituximab for refractory cytopenias associated with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS).
evidence.snippet: Toxicities including hypogammaglobulinemia and neutropenia constitute an additional infection risk burden

nodes.description: mTOR inhibition with sirolimus is an effective steroid-sparing therapy for refractory autoimmune cytopenias and benign lymphoproliferation in ALPS, and can normalize the double-negative T-cell compartment.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment with sirolimus results in complete responses in patients with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Four patients were treated for autoimmune cytopenias; all had a rapid complete or near complete response.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment with sirolimus results in complete responses in patients with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Three patients had complete resolution of lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly and all patients had a reduction in double negative T cells, a population hallmark of the disease.

evidence.reference_title: Sirolimus is effective in relapsed/refractory autoimmune cytopenias: results of a prospective multi-institutional trial.
evidence.snippet: All children (N = 12) with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) achieved a durable complete response (CR), including rapid improvement in autoimmune disease, lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly within 1 to 3 months of starting sirolimus.

nodes.description: In selected ALPS patients, treatment for refractory cytopenias can produce prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia after B-cell depletion, while splenectomy creates an asplenia-associated sepsis risk.
nodes.id: Treatment-related humoral immune suppression and asplenia-associated infection vulnerability
